entry.title: Error KI154 in Manage My Timesheet: How to Resolve Activity Type and Cost Center Issues
entry.content:
In this blog post, I would like to share an issue we encountered in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud that prevented users from recording their working time using the Manage My Timesheet app. This article may help others facing similar problems related to error KI154.

 

Issue:

Some users were unable to record their time in the Manage My Timesheet app. After creating a timesheet entry, the following error message was displayed: 

  • Enter activity type only in conjunction with cost center (KI154) (in English)
  • Geben Sie die Leistungsart nur in Verbindung mit einer Kostenstelle an (KI154)  (in German)

 

 

 

Root Cause:

Due to the setup of the interface between SAP S/4HANA and the HR system, each master data change created a new record while removing the cost center and manager from the previous one. When users attempted to record time for an older time period, that record no longer contained a cost center, which triggered error KI154.

 

Solution:

To resolve the issue, perform the following steps: 
1. Open the Manage Workforce app. 
2. Check the employee’s Work Agreement. 
3. Ensure that a Cost Center is maintained for the relevant time period. 
4. If required, temporarily reverse the changes that removed the cost center. 
5. After the timesheet entries have been recorded, the changes can be applied again.

 

Summary:

Error KI154 occurs when a user records time for a period in which no cost center is assigned in the employee master data. Ensuring that the cost center is correctly maintained for the relevant time slot will resolve the issue. 

References:

  • 2647776 - Manage My Timesheet - Error Enter activity type only in conjunction with cost center. - SAP for Me
  • Solved: In Manage My Timesheet unable to Create Task - SAP Community

entry.title: Custom Business Configurations (F4579): Collaborative draft
entry.content:
Introduction

The Custom Business Configurations (CUBCO) app serves as an entry point to the Business Configuration Maintenance Object (SMBC) provided by custom applications or partners.

When you use the ADT wizard to generate a Business Configuration Maintenance Object, the singleton pattern is used when generating the draft enabled RAP BO. One characteristic of this pattern is that all child entities are locked together, so it is not possible for multiple users to work with the same configuration at the same time.

This blog describes how to avoid this restriction by using collaborative draft.

This blog is relevant for

  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition (2025)
  • SAP BTP ABAP environment 
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 

Further reading:

  • Related blog posts
  • Learn how you can use ABAP technology to develop innovative applications and business solutions across SAP’s portfolio on SAP Learning Site.

Example scenario

In the configuration table ZDEMO_CC, the company codes are stored.

@EndUserText.label : 'Company Code'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #C
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #ALLOWED
define table zdemo_cc {
  key client            : abap.clnt not null;
  key company_code      : abap.numc(4) not null;
  country               : land1;
  last_changed_at       : abp_lastchange_tstmpl;
  local_last_changed_at : abp_locinst_lastchange_tstmpl;
}

Enable collaborative draft

In the generated behavior definition, add "collaborative" to the draft annotation:

managed with additional save implementation in class ZBP_I_COMPANYCODE_S unique;
strict;
with collaborative draft;

Add the draft query annotation and the share action to the singleton entity definition:

define behavior for ZI_CompanyCode_S alias CompanyCodeAll
draft table ZDEMO_CC_D_S query ZR_DEMO_CC_CD
[...]
{
  draft action Share;
  [...]
}

 Right-click the singleton draft table and choose New Data Definition to create the CDS view for the draft query:

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Draft Query View'
define view entity ZR_DEMO_CC_CD
  as select from zdemo_cc_d_s
{
  key singletonid                   as Singletonid,
      lastchangedatmax              as Lastchangedatmax,
      transportrequestid            as Transportrequestid,
      draftentitycreationdatetime   as Draftentitycreationdatetime,
      draftentitylastchangedatetime as Draftentitylastchangedatetime,
      draftadministrativedatauuid   as Draftadministrativedatauuid,
      draftentityoperationcode      as Draftentityoperationcode,
      hasactiveentity               as Hasactiveentity,
      draftfieldchanges             as Draftfieldchanges
}

You need to enhance the authorization of the involved users. In SAP BTP ABAP environment and SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Edition you assign business catalog SAP_CORE_BC_RAP_DRAFT_PC to the user, in SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition authorization object S_DRAFT.

Collaboration

User TB is creating a new company code 400 while at the same time user PW is editing company code 100. All changes are recorded on the same transport request.

Since the collaborative draft is enabled for the singleton root entity, the save and discard draft action are applied to all object changes. In this example, users PW and TB process two different company codes, which are logically different objects. Technically, however, they process the same singleton root entity. In practice, this means that discarding the draft or saving must be aligned between the users involved, as the save action persists the changes in all company codes.
For completely independent editing of individual objects, you must develop a RAP BO without a singleton pattern.

entry.title: User Assistance Is Here To Help You!
entry.content:
Since then, we’ve been at work making the best use of the feedback that you gave us, so we wanted very shortly to do just a couple of things here.

First, offer a hearty and sincere THANK YOU for the feedback during and after the session, as well as whenever you take the time out of your busy day to give it. We are always grateful for your perspective as we work to refine and improve what we’re doing for you.

Second, we wanted to reiterate the open call for feedback: Whether it’s via reply to this post, questions or comments on the SAP Community that you tag one of us in, or, maybe best of all, comments left using the feedback tool on the SAP Help Portal, we’re always here to help.

In fact, we've had numerous conversations or other direct feedback about things that are important to you that we've been working on, and are always happy to hear more.

So if you’ve got something on your mind—about a Help Portal topic, or some in-app help content that isn’t cutting it for you, or a question about any What’s New content, or, really, anything, use that feedback feature, comment here, or reach out to us.

We’ll make sure the feedback gets where it needs to and that we’ll get to work on it!

Thank you!

entry.title: Discover the Power of Cloud ERP: Bringing Your Business into the Next Software Era
entry.content:
Over the past years, we have witnessed remarkable technological advances: from the rise of Software as a Service (SaaS) to the rapid adoption of artificial intelligence across the software industry. These innovations are reshaping the next generation of business solutions and revolutionising how companies will operate in the decades ahead. 

Today’s technologies empower organization with faster insights, higher efficiency, and new ways to differentiate and innovate. They boost productivity and support better, data driven decisions. 

SAP Cloud ERP: The Next Generation of Intelligent Business Software 

With more than 50 years of industry expertise, SAP is delivering a new generation of business solutions through SAP Cloud ERP. This modern platform combines proven best practices with intelligent technologies to help companies achieve greater efficiency and stronger business outcomes. 

SAP Cloud ERP is a SaaS (Software as a Service) based solution enhanced with embedded AI capabilities. It offers modern and intelligent end to end processes across finance, procurement, sales, service, supply chain, and manufacturing. These processes are delivered as industry best practices, enabling faster implementations and providing a stable, controlled approach for extending the system with custom developments. 

Users benefit from an intuitive, launchpad based experience and the power of Joule, SAP’s intelligent assistant, which accelerates task completion and supports knowledge enrichment. Integrated AI features further enhance daily operations and improve business insights. 

Flexibility to Extend and Innovate 

If the standard end to end processes do not fully meet your business needs, SAP Cloud ERP offers extensive flexibility: 

  • Out of the box integrations with other SAP and third party solutions 
  • Hundreds of APIs available via the SAP API Hub to tailor your processes 
  • SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) to build custom extensions and further innovate according to your unique requirements 

This platform ensures your organization can grow and evolve without compromising stability or/and scalability. 

 How to transform to SAP Cloud ERP  

Customers can start this transformation journey leveraging the SAP integrated toolchain with SAP Signavio Process Insights (optional) and/ or Digital Discovery Assessment – DDA,  focusing either their own historical data or processes or their business processes as a starting point. It is a fact-based approach for the transformation.  

Are you curious to understand more about our next software generation Cloud ERP and how to move into this journey? I welcome you to join us on our next webinar on  April 14, 2026 about Innovation, Scope & Standard Business Processes. You can register here: https://events.sap.com/transformation/en_us/home.html .

entry.title: SAP Sustainability Footprint Management: Q1-26 Updates & Highlights
entry.content:
As we continue into 2026, I’m back with the next edition of my quarterly blog series covering the latest innovations and highlights in SAP Sustainability Footprint Management. Over the last quarter, we’ve extended existing capabilities, introduced new ones, and invested further in usability, integration, and enablement – all with the goal of helping our customers calculate, analyze, and operationalize sustainability footprints at scale, grounded in ERP data. In this edition, I’m covering:

  • Transport Footprint Enhancements
  • Customer‑Relevant Transport Footprints (Scope 3.9)
  • Distance Adjustment Factors
  • Transport Analytics
  • Categorize Scope 3.2 Footprints
  • Usability Improvements
  • Other Release Highlights
  • Unibloom Emission Factor Datasets
  • Multi‑Impact Footprints in Practice: Learning Journey, FLAG and TfS Use Cases
  • Additional Information

Transport Footprint Enhancements

The last quarter brought several enhancements to the ERP‑centric transport footprint calculation capabilities in SAP Sustainability Footprint Management. These updates extend coverage to customer-relevant transport footprints, including integration of customer master data, give you more control over the distance calculations used for transport footprints, and provide you with comprehensive analytical capabilities.

Customer‑Relevant Transport Footprints (Scope 3.9)

In one of my previous posts, I introduced ERP‑centric transport footprint calculation for upstream and internal transport, covering Greenhouse Gas Protocol Scope 3.4 emissions. This capability has now been extended to the downstream side of the value chain, enabling the calculation of customer‑relevant transport footprints and, with that, Scope 3.9 (Downstream Transport and Distribution) emissions.

The calculation also leverages existing ERP business transaction data and builds on the same transport modeling approach used for upstream transport. The foundation of this enhancement is the introduction of a Customer role for business partners. Business partners with this role, including address data, can be replicated directly from integrated SAP S/4HANA source systems, through APIs, or imported via file in the Import Master Data app. The system derives customer‑relevant transport activities from the following business transactions:

  • Goods Issue for Customer (Outbound deliveries of products to customers)
  • Customer Return (Goods returned by customers to company locations).

When business transactions are replicated from integrated source systems, these transactions now include the Customer ID, ensuring correct assignment of customer‑relevant transport activities during transport footprint calculation. In this context, targeted enhancements were made to the Import Master Data and Import Business Transactions OData APIs on SAP Business Accelerator Hub.

Based on the configurations in the Manage Transport Settings app, SAP Sustainability Footprint Management automatically generates transport activities, determines routes and legs, and calculates footprints per activity, route, and leg, applying the relevant emission factors by mode of transport. The resulting Scope 3.9 emissions are seamlessly integrated into both Corporate Carbon Footprint (CCF) and Product Carbon Footprint (PCF) calculations and are available in the standard footprint result and analytical apps – all grounded in ERP data rather than manual estimates.

For setup and process details, see Calculating Customer‑Relevant Transport Footprints Using Business Transactions in SAP Help. For importing business partners and business transactions, check Configurations for Importing Master Data and Configurations for Importing Business Transaction Data.

Customer Relevant Transport Footprints (Scope 3.9)

Distance Adjustment Factors

Transport footprint calculations in SAP Sustainability Footprint Management derive distances between locations automatically using the Great Circle Distance (GCD) method. Since real transport routes often deviate from the shortest path due to road networks, infrastructure, and routing constraints, GCD can underestimate the actual distance traveled. To reflect this, you can now configure distance adjustment factors for different modes of transport in the Manage Transport Settings app. When routes and legs are generated, the system applies the configured adjustment factor to the GCD, resulting in a more realistic distance basis for footprint calculations.

Before configuring distance adjustment factors, you should review the methodology documentation of your emission factor sources. Some emission factor datasets already include distance adjustments – for example, GLEC v3.1 emission factors for air transport. Applying an additional adjustment factor in such cases would lead to incorrect results.

Also note that existing transport activities aren’t updated automatically. To apply new distance adjustment factors, routes and legs must be regenerated and the relevant transport footprints recalculated. For details, check SAP Help: Configuring Distance Adjustment Factors and Generating Routes and Legs.

Configure Distance Adjustment Factors

Transport Analytics

To complement the transport footprint calculation capabilities, a new analytics app called Transport is now available in the Analytics section of the SAP Fiori launchpad. The app provides a comprehensive, map‑based view of corporate and product transport footprints for a selected footprint inventory scope and period. Transport legs are visualized as connections between source and target locations, with color‑coding that highlights higher‑emission legs. You can filter by product, mode of transport, business transaction type, or location, and switch to a table view for detailed, leg‑level analysis sorted by emission value. The app also supports comparisons across different periods. To use the app, you need the SUS_PFM_Calculate_Transport_Footprints_Read role. See SAP Help for more information: Analyzing Footprints for Transport.

Transport Analytics

To reflect all those updates, the process steps for calculating transport footprints using business transaction data have also been updated in the Scenario Guide: Process 8 – Calculating Scope 3.4 and Scope 3.9 Emissions Using Business Transaction Data.

 

Categorize Scope 3.2 Footprints

Until now, all purchased product footprints covered in the Manage Purchased Product Footprints app were automatically attributed to GHG Protocol Scope 3.1 (Purchased Goods and Services) in corporate carbon footprint calculations. For most companies, this is the correct assignment – but not in all cases. Capital goods such as machinery, equipment, or buildings that are purchased and used over multiple years belong to Scope 3.2 (Capital Goods). Correctly distinguishing between Scope 3.1 and Scope 3.2 is important both for GHG Protocol compliance and for maintaining a high‑quality footprint inventory. This is also a feature that was requested by customers via SAP Influence.

With a recent enhancement, you can now explicitly assign a purchased product footprint or product group footprint to either:

  • Scope 3.1 – Purchased Goods and Services, or
  • Scope 3.2 – Capital Goods

This categorization is supported consistently across all relevant workflows in SAP Sustainability Footprint Management:

  • When creating purchased product footprints manually,
  • When mapping emission factors manually or using AI‑based mapping, and
  • When importing purchased product footprints via data files.

Scope 3.2 Categorization

This enhancement ensures that each purchased product footprint is correctly attributed when calculating your organizational footprint inventory, resulting in a more accurate and auditable split between Scope 3.1 and Scope 3.2. Existing purchased product footprints that were previously categorized automatically remain assigned to Scope 3.1 by default, ensuring backward compatibility. For more information, see Creating Footprints for Purchased Products and Product Groups in SAP Help.

For analyzing your calculated footprints, the updated categorization is also reflected correctly in the Footprint Overview app, where purchased product footprints assigned to Scope 3.2 are shown separately from Scope 3.1.

 

Usability Improvements

To support more efficient day‑to‑day work with SAP Sustainability Footprint Management, we continuously invest in improving usability and increasing automation. Last quarter, we introduced several enhancements around form‑based data collection, manual data imports, and better transparency in integrations. These improvements focus on reducing manual effort in recurring data collection tasks, simplifying bulk operations, and making changes and corrections easier to manage at scale – especially in scenarios with large numbers of forms, users, or integrated systems. Many of these enhancements are the direct result of customer feedback, collected through SAP Influence as well as ongoing conversations with customers and partners.

You can now change the status of GHG data forms directly during data import when using spreadsheet templates in the Record GHG Data and Manage GHG Data Collections apps. GHG forms can be set to Applicable or Not Applicable as part of the import. When setting a form to Not Applicable, a comment is required, consistent with the behavior in the user interface. This applies to both bulk templates on the main page of the apps and the individual templates per GHG form type, streamlining bulk updates and reducing manual follow‑up steps.

To further automate and improve correction workflows, GHG data forms that were previously set to Completed or Not Applicable can now be reopened via API using the POST/massUpdate action in the GHG Data Forms API on SAP Business Accelerator Hub. This provides additional flexibility for automated correction scenarios and integration workflows where reported data needs to be revised after submission.

For providing a more consistent and efficient user experience, downloaded templates now better reflect the UI and applied filters. Template sorting now mirrors the UI table sorting, and templates can be downloaded together with the currently applied UI filters. These enhancements apply to apps such as Manage Planned Energy Consumption Rates, Manage Business Activities, Model Energy Flows, Manage Waste Footprints, Record GHG Data, Manage Transport Operation Footprints, Manage Allocations, Manage Purchased Product Footprints, and Manage Transport Settings. In the Calculate Footprints, Model Energy Flows, Record GHG Data, and Manage GHG Data Collections apps, downloading templates together with the displayed data is now the default behavior.

Finally, the sustainability data integration apps now include a Change History tab in Manage Replications, Manage Source Systems, and Configure Extensions for Replications. This allows you to track who made which changes and when, including the old and new values for edited fields, improving transparency and traceability when managing replication configurations.

 

Other Release Highlights

When performing a current period calculation, input items for footprint items of type Product that only contribute a defined CO₂e value without a product quantity are no longer considered in the per‑unit calculation. These input items are excluded because they have no associated product quantity and would otherwise distort per‑unit results, for example transports or allocations. Their CO₂e values are still fully aggregated into the total footprint. When using CO₂e‑only input items, make sure they’re scaled to the total product quantity where appropriate.

The system now supports goods receipt from production even without production history such as goods issues to production, work‑in‑progress postings, or internal service confirmations in the current period. In these cases, SAP Sustainability Footprint Management uses the previous period footprint per unit of the produced product or, if not available, maintained purchased product footprints. This eliminates previous calculation errors and applies the product footprint determination methods defined in the calculation variant.

Custom temperature conditions can now be replicated from source systems using the Manage Replications app. This applies to SAP S/4HANA and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition source systems. For pull‑based replication scenarios, the setup requires custom CDS views in the source system.

Lastly, we’re also continuously enhancing our documentation on SAP Help to improve transparency and onboarding. The Implement – Getting Started section now includes a link to ERP Objects, providing details on relevant CDS views, tables, and fields in SAP S/4HANA for each supported master data entity. In addition, the Product Carbon Footprint Scenario Guide has been extended with step‑by‑step processes for calculating product carbon footprints for cradle‑to‑gate and cradle‑to‑grave scenarios, including concrete examples for allocating Scope 1, 2, and 3 emissions using business activities, allocations, and business transaction data.

 

Unibloom Emission Factor Datasets

One of the persistent challenges in Scope 3 reporting is the gap between supplier‑specific data and the global average emission factors that many companies fall back on when that data isn’t available. Those averages can be misleading. For agricultural commodities, for instance, emission factors can vary by up to 200% depending on the country of origin, reflecting differences in land‑use change, energy consumption, and local production practices. The wider the gap between your actual supply chain and the averages you apply, the less reliable your footprint calculations become. To overcome this challenge, we onboarded a new addition to the SAP partner ecosystem: Unibloom. Unibloom’s country‑specific supply‑chain emissions datasets are now available on SAP Store and can be integrated directly with SAP Sustainability Footprint Management.

Unibloom provides ISO 14044‑aligned, country‑specific emission factors for agricultural and industrial commodities, including land‑use‑ and management‑adjusted data. The coverage spans key sectors such as SBTi FLAG (Forest, Land, and Agriculture). The datasets are delivered in a ready‑to‑import format and can be extended on demand with additional country‑specific factors for specific materials, ingredients, or industries. With Unibloom datasets in place, you can replace broad global averages with granular, location‑specific emission factors in your footprint calculations. This improves accuracy and auditability, increases confidence in reported results, and supports more informed sourcing and procurement decisions.

To go deeper into the datasets and their practical use with SAP Sustainability Footprint Management, we’re hosting a joint webinar with Unibloom. The session focuses on improving Scope 3 accuracy for agricultural and FLAG‑relevant value chains and shows how country‑specific emission factors can be applied in practice. Access the webinar and register here: End data paralysis. Know when your Scope 3 data is good enough.

 

Multi‑Impact Footprints in Practice: Learning Journey, FLAG and TfS Use Cases

To support our customers in moving beyond pure carbon metrics, we introduced multi‑impact environmental footprints in SAP Sustainability Footprint Management last quarter – I covered the core capability in more detail in my deep dive.

To help you apply this capability in practice, consistently across different reporting frameworks and industry requirements, we’ve invested in further enablement resources. As a starting point, a new Learning Journey provides a structured introduction to the topic: Establishing the Multi-impact Environmental Footprint with SAP Sustainability Footprint Management. It walks through the underlying concepts, configuration, and usage of multi‑impact footprints, providing the context for how additional environmental impact categories can be calculated, managed, and analyzed alongside carbon, using a single, consistent data model.

With the basics in place, the focus is now shifting from how multi‑impact categories work to how they can be applied in practice for concrete reporting frameworks and industry requirements. In this context, my colleague @Cielo_SanabriaRuiz has published two follow‑up posts that demonstrate exactly that:

The first, SBTi Forest, Land, and Agriculture (FLAG) in SAP Sustainability Footprint Management, explains why distinguishing land‑based emissions from fossil emissions matters at both corporate and product level. It walks through the specific impact categories and emission factors required for SBTi FLAG compliance and includes a mapping table that links impact categories to FLAG reporting categories.

The second, SAP Sustainability Footprint Management for the Chemicals (Together for Sustainability), focuses on the chemical industry and the Together for Sustainability (TfS) guideline. It covers the sector‑specific setup for product carbon footprints and Scope 3.1 calculations, including allocation rules for multi‑output processes, transport requirements such as the Radiative Forcing Index (RFI) for air transport, and a complete mapping of impact categories to TfS reporting standards.

Taken together, this enablement material shows how multi‑impact footprints can be used to support FLAG and TfS reporting requirements without fragmenting data models or calculation logic, while still staying aligned with established standards. If you’re working on FLAG reporting, chemical‑industry footprinting, or more broadly on extending footprint calculations beyond carbon, these resources provide a coherent path from concepts to practical application.

 

Additional Information

For in-depth details on SAP Sustainability Footprint Management, including concepts, setup, and the application help, I recommend you browsing through the SAP Help Portal. For an overview of all new features and functions that have been released, check the What’s New section, which is updated with every new software release. To continue receiving e-mail notifications, you need to subscribe again – just visit the page and choose Subscribe.

For exploring and experimenting in a live system, register for the free SAP Sustainability Footprint Management Trial. You’ll receive an e-mail with access information within seconds after your registration and can then test the solution at your own convenience within a 30-day trial period. It also includes a wide range of guided tours designed to help you navigate through all the features and functions comprehensively. Recently, we also added new tours and will shortly release additional ones, representing the latest features and an enhanced user experience.

To stay up to date on SAP Sustainability Footprint Management, follow me here in the SAP Community. In regular cadence, I’ll publish blog posts giving you updates on all the product’s new features and functions as well as deep dives into the product capabilities. I’m also looking forward to engaging with you directly, feel free to leave your feedback in the comment section.

entry.title: Javascript Date function and dynamic coloring
entry.content:
While working in SAP adobe form, developers come across a situation where they need to calculate the arrears based on the creation date and payment date of a document. Also, there could be some requirements to color a particular cell based on the values populated. With the help of Javascript we can achieve these requirements in on premise system as well as on public cloud. 

In an on premise system the arrear days calculation can be handled using the ABAP code but while working in SAP Public cloud we need to make use of scripting language to achieve such requirements. 
In the below example I have demonstrated to use the Date function in javascript to calculate the arrears. Also the dynamic coloring of cells based on the value populated.

To demonstrate the example I have taken invoices from invoice table DFKKINVDOC_H.

Step 1: Create an adobe form interface in SFP T-code and do the relevant data select. Data can be selected as per the business requirements, but here for the simplicity I have selected all the invoices corresponding to a customer.

 

Step 2: Create the form layout in SFP T-Code and use the interface created in the previous step.

 

Step 3: I have added a column called as Arrears to calculate the date difference between the invoice creation date and net due date. 
Let's first generate the pdf and see the current output. 

Step 4: Now, we need to calculate the arrear days between the dates. We can do it using ABAP also but javascript helps us in public cloud.
To do so, select the Arrears column and then select the Calculate event and also select the Language as Javascript.

Step 5: Let's write the Javascript Code to calculate the difference between the dates using date function.

Now, when we have written the code in javascript, let's generate the pdf now and see if the data is coming for Arrears.

( Please ignore some of the values here as it is a development system and it can have data discrepancies. Our purpose is to use the Date function to calculate the difference )

var creationDate = this.parent.CRDATE.rawValue  // Creation date of the record
var dueDate = this.parent.FAEDN.rawValue;        // Invoice due date
// Split the raw value and get the correct date format. JS has this format YYYY-MM-DDT00:00:00
var invoiceCrDate = (creationDate.split("T")[0]).split("-");  // -> This will convert date format from YYYY-MM-DDT00:00:00 to YYYY-MM-DD
var invoiceDueDate = (dueDate.split("T")[0]).split("-");
// Get the Date, Month and Year individually for invoice Creation Date
var invCrYear = parseInt(invoiceCrDate[0]);
var invCrMonth = parseInt(invoiceCrDate[1]) - 1;  // Javascript Months are 0 index based and hence -1 is required
var invCrDate = parseInt(invoiceCrDate[2]);
// Get the Date, Month and Year individually for invoice Due Date
var invDueYear = parseInt(invoiceDueDate[0]);
var invDueMonth = parseInt(invoiceDueDate[1]) - 1;  // Javascript Months are 0 index based and hence -1 is required
var invDueDate = parseInt(invoiceDueDate[2]);
// Create a new Date object to find the difference
var startDate = new Date(invCrYear, invCrMonth, invCrDate, 00, 00, 00, 00);
var endDate = new Date(invDueYear, invDueMonth, invDueDate, 00, 00, 00, 00);
// Calculate the values of one day
var oneDay = 24 * 60 * 60 * 1000;  // here multiply with 1000 else the value will come in decimal
// calculate the difference
this.rawValue = Math.round((startDate - endDate) / oneDay);

Let's proceed further to color the cells accordingly. For example, we will color the cell with red if the arrear days is less than 0, color the cell with green if the arrear days is greater than 0, and keep the cell color as it is if the arrear days is equal to 0.

Step 6: First we need to find the RGB value of colors which are going to be used. We can find it easily on the internet. For our case we need RGB value of Red and Green color. 

( Based on your requirement and color combination, you can find the rgb value accordingly )
Red -> rgb(255, 0, 0)
Green -> rgb(0, 255, 0)

Just add the below additional code in the previous code( code written in step 5 )

// Let's assume that the arrears' cell with value greater than 0 should be colored with green color 
// And the cell value less than 0 should be colored with red and for 0 no color
if ( this.rawValue < 0 ){
      this.fillColor = "255,0,0", this.rawValue;
}
else if ( this.rawValue > 0 ){
       this.fillColor = "0,255,0", this.rawValue;
}



Step 7: Now, let's generate the pdf once again and see the output.

With the help of above steps we can achieve our arrear calculation as well as dynamic coloring of cell according to the value. This is one of the use cases of Date function in Javascript, you can have different scenarios to use the date function.

** One small bonus trick in JS, many of you might be knowing but this is also a kind of common requirements. The user may ask the developer to put the symbol of currency dynamically in the amount field **

Step 8 :  Select the Amount field and select the calculate event and then write the below code.

 

// Write the symbol of currency based on the recods.
var currKey = this.parent.TOTAL_CURR.rawValue;
if ( currKey == 'EUR' ){
      this.format.picture.value = "num{'€' z,zzz,zzz,zz9.99}", this.rawValue;
   }
else if ( currKey == 'USD' ){
      this.format.picture.value = "num{'$' z,zzz,zzz,zz9.99}", this.rawValue;
}
else {
      this.format.picture.value = "num{z,zzz,zzz,zz9.99}", this.rawValue;
}

 

Step 9: Let's generate the pdf once again and see the current output.

You may want to minimize the width of Curreny field( in our scenario it is the last column if you intent to show only the amount with symbol ).

Please let know if you have any other solutions for the above mentioned requirements. It will be helpful to all the ABAP developers.

Happy Learning !!!

Thanks,
Shubham

entry.title: Conditions Not Saved After First Entry in Purchase Contract
entry.content:
In this blog post, I would like to share an issue we encountered in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud where where pricing conditions are not saved after being entered for the first time in a purchase contract in the App Manage Purchase Contracts. This may help others facing similar inconsistencies in condition handling.

 

Issue:

In one of our test scenarios, condition DRV1 was maintained in a purchase contract.

The steps were as follows:

  1. Open the purchase contract in Manage Purchase Contracts.
  2. Navigate to the conditions section.
  3. Enter condition DRV1.
  4. Save the contract.

At first glance, everything seemed fine — no error messages were shown during the save process.
However, when checking the Purchase Contract Output, the condition was not printed here. And when reopening the purchase contract afterward, the condition DRV1 was no longer visible and appeared to be missing entirely.

Interestingly, when the same condition DRV1 was entered a second time, followed by saving the contract again, the condition is then stored correctly and remains visible when reopening the document.

 

Analysis:

At first, this behavior looked like a system defect or an issue with condition determination to us. Since there are no error messages and the save action completes successfully, the expectation was that the condition data has been stored.

However, the fact that the condition was only saved after re-entering it a second time suggests that the system never actually registered the initial input as confirmed data.

 

Root Cause:

The root cause of the issue is missing confirmation of the condition entry. SAP Support confirmed that the system was showing its' expected behaviour here.

When maintaining conditions, the entered condition must be explicitly confirmed by pressing Enter.
If the user enters the condition and immediately saves the document without pressing Enter, the system does not fully recognize the condition line as final input — even though it appears on the screen.

As a result, the condition is not transferred to the document data and is therefore not persisted during save.

 

Solution:

To ensure that conditions are saved correctly:

✅Confirm the condition with the Enter key before saving.

Once the condition has been confirmed with Enter, it will be saved correctly and will remain visible when the contract is reopened and will also be printed on the generated output.

 

Summary:

If you encounter situations where conditions or other values appear to be “lost” after saving, always check whether the input was properly confirmed beforehand by using the Enter key before saving.

entry.title: Your Sherlock Homes – How to Segregate Configuration from Releasing Transports
entry.content:
Co-Author: Lisa Kouch

Introduction

Recently I got a question from a customer: In the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, we usually give BPC Expert business role to a user who does the CBC and system configurations. However, to segregate the responsibility, we don’t want that configuration user to be able to release transports; we want to add a layer of control for the internal control purpose.  Only the transport releaser can release a transport. How can I achieve this?

If the purpose of this responsibility lies in the approval and documentation purpose (aka process control), my blog Using SAP CALM Features App to Track Transports in the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition should serve the purpose.  However, if you want to know the details on controlling this responsibility in the system, this blog discusses the segregation of configurations from releasing transports.

Check Out the Business Catalogs

If you are not familiar with the user management concept in the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, please refer to my blog User Management in a Nutshell for the SAP S/4HANA Cloud, public edition.

As always, my first step of research is the infamous SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library.  Since I want to control who can release the configuration transports, let me check out the app Export Customizing Transports.  By selecting the IMPLEMENTATION INFORMATION tab, then expanding the Configuration section, we can find the important information as below:

Access Control of App Export Customizing Transports

There are three Business Catalogs control the access (disregard two deprecated ones):

  • SAP_CORE_BC_BCT_TRN_DSP_PC for Transport Display
  • SAP_CORE_BC_BCT_TRN_MNG_PC for Transport Management
  • SAP_CORE_BC_BCT_TRN_REL_PC for Transport Release Management

My idea is to only give the Configurator the Business Catalogs SAP_CORE_BC_BCT_TRN_DSP_PC and SAP_CORE_BC_BCT_TRN_MNG_PC so that he/she cannot release the transports. If you take out the SAP_CORE_BC_BCT_TRN_MNG_PC, the configurator cannot create a transport which is not what we want.

Next, I found out two Business Roles might contain these three business catalogs:

  • SAP_BR_ADMINISTRATOR
  • SAP_BR_BPC_EXPERT

To verify my idea, I need to get into the system to check the Business Role derived from the template SAP_BR_BPC_EXPERT.  In my case, it is the Z_BR_BPC_EXPERT.  All Three transport related business catalogs are there.

Three Transport Related Business Catalogs in the Business Role Z_BR_BPC_EXPERT

Testing

When all three business catalogs were assigned to the business role Z_BR_BPC_EXPERT, I created a dummy (empty) configuration transport.  Then I take out the business catalog SAP_CORE_BC_BCT_TRN_REL_PC.  When opening the app Export Customizing Transports, the Release button is grayed out. Therefore, I achieved the objective: the configuration user can view but cannot release the transport.  Furthermore, since the business catalog SAP_CORE_BC_BCT_TRN_MNG_PC is assigned, the user can still manage the transport, such as creating a transport, assigning a transport to him/herself, delete a transport, etc.

Without Business Catalog SAP_CORE_BC_BCT_TRN_REL_PC, the User Cannot Release a Transport

Recommendations

To handle the situation described in this blog, I recommend using an adapted business role from an SAP template. You can add or remove necessary business catalogs as needed.  For the transport releaser, you won’t give him/her the BPC Expert business role.  You can create a new role with very limited business catalog such as the SAP_CORE_BC_BCT_TRN_REL_PC and assign to him/her.  For the configurator, you should remove the business catalog SAP_CORE_BC_BCT_TRN_REL_PC from any business roles assigned to him/her.  In particular, we know that the business role derived from the SAP Template SAP_BR_ADMINISTRATOR includes the business catalog SAP_CORE_BC_BCT_TRN_REL_PC as shown below.  Therefore, don’t give the system configurator the administration business role.  Just be thorough when doing your investigation.

Two Transports Related Business Catalogs in the SAP Template SAP_BR_ADMINISTRATOR

Conclusion

By removing one business catalog SAP_CORE_BC_BCT_TRN_REL_PC usually assigned to the configurators as a BPC Expert, you can segregate the configuration from transport releases.

References

  • Blog: Using SAP CALM Features App to Track Transports in the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
  • Blog: User Management in a Nutshell for the SAP S/4HANA Cloud, public edition

entry.title: Changing the Fiscal Year Variant in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition
entry.content:
What You Need to Know Before Requesting a Fiscal Period Variant Change

Changing the fiscal year definition in a productive SAP S/4HANA Cloud, public edition system is a rare but critical requirement—typically driven by major business events such as company sales, mergers, demergers, or acquisitions.

This blog post explains why fiscal year variant changes are complex, what scenarios are supported, and how customers can proceed safely and compliantly using SAP’s recommended approach.

Business Context: Why Companies Need to Change Their Fiscal Year Variant

Customers usually request a fiscal year or fiscal period change when new business conditions arise, for example:

  • Sale of a company or business unit
  • Merger or acquisition
  • Division or carve‑out of an existing entity
  • Alignment with a new group reporting calendar

These changes often impact how financial reporting periods are structured—and therefore require changes to the fiscal year variant.

Common Scenarios

  1. Introducing a Shortened Fiscal Year

A typical example is a transition from a calendar fiscal year (January–December) to a non‑calendar fiscal year (July–June).

In this case:

  • The old fiscal year ends in December
  • The new fiscal year starts in July
  • A shortened fiscal year is required to bridge the period January–June

This avoids gaps or overlaps in financial reporting.

  1. Changing Fiscal Posting Period Definitions

Another common requirement is changing posting period structures, for example:

  • From calendar months to a 4‑4‑5 calendar
  • Or to 13 posting periods based on four‑week cycles

Although the fiscal year start/end dates may remain the same, the fiscal period definition must be adjusted accordingly.

Why Fiscal Year Variants Cannot Be Changed Directly in Production

In SAP S/4HANA Cloud, public edition, it is not possible to change the fiscal year variant retrospectively in a productive system.

The key reasons are:

🔹Data Integrity and Dependencies

Fiscal year information is deeply embedded in financial data such as:

  • Journal entries
  • Actuals and planning data
  • Universal Journal tables (for example, ACDOCA, ACDOCP)

Changing the fiscal year definition directly would risk inconsistencies in existing transaction data.

🔹Cross‑Application Impact

The fiscal year variant is used across multiple application areas:

  • General Ledger Accounting
  • Asset Accounting
  • Controlling
  • Reporting

Any change affects the entire financial landscape, not just one configuration object.

🔹Customer‑Specific Complexity

Each customer has a unique setup:

  • Ledger assignments
  • Reporting requirements
  • Audit constraints

This makes a standardized, self‑service change technically and operationally infeasible.

The Supported Approach: SAP DMLT Consulting Service

For SAP S/4HANA Cloud, public edition, changing the fiscal year definition or fiscal period structure requires a customer‑specific DMLT (Data Management & Landscape Transformation) consulting Service.

The consulting service typically includes:

  • Impact analysis
  • Configuration of the new fiscal year variant
  • Data conversion where required
  • Support for customer‑specific execution steps
  • Coordination with audit and compliance requirements

This applies whether you:

  • Introduce a shortened fiscal year, or
  • Change only the fiscal period (posting period) definition

Important Scope and Restrictions

Please consider the following constraints when planning:

  • ✅Only one single fiscal year variant assigned across all ledgers and company codes can be changed
  • ✅Supported only for SAP Central Business Configuration–managed implementations
  • ✅The change impacts future data only
  • ❌Historical transaction data is out of scope

Technical Note

If a preconfigured fiscal year variant was used during the initial implementation:

  • A new customer‑specific fiscal year variant ID must be created
  • All data containing the old variant ID (for example, line items in ACDOCA) must be converted as part of the project

How to Request the Change

To initiate the process:

  1. Plan early and ensure sufficient lead time
  2. Align with internal stakeholders and your auditor
  3. Create a case under component CA‑LT‑SRV‑2CL
  4. Engage with SAP DMLT Consulting Service to define scope, timeline, and execution strategy

Early engagement significantly reduces risk and helps ensure a smooth transition.

Key Takeaways

  • Changing a fiscal year in SAP S/4HANA Cloud is not a configuration activity, but a controlled transformation
  • Shortened fiscal years and fiscal period changes are fully supported—with expert guidance
  • Early planning, audit alignment, and SAP DMLT involvement are critical for success
  • 3678328 - Change of Fiscal Year Variant in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

If you have gone through a fiscal year change in SAP S/4HANA Cloud or are planning one, feel free to share your experience or questions in the comments. Collaboration and shared learnings make these complex topics easier for everyone.

entry.title: Preserving Critical Supplier Information: Transferring of Supplier Material Number (IDNLF)
entry.content:
Preserving Critical Supplier Information:

Why Transferring the Supplier Material Number (IDNLF) from Purchase Requisition to Purchase Order Matters

In SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, procurement processes are designed to be efficient, guided, and seamlessly integrated. From purchase requisition creation to purchase order execution, data consistency is key to ensuring smooth operational flows.

However, in everyday procurement scenarios, even small gaps in data transfer can lead to unnecessary effort, loss of information, and reduced user confidence. One such gap exists today in the handling of the Supplier Material Number (IDNLF) during the transition from Purchase Requisition (PR) to Purchase Order (PO).

________________________________________________________________________________________________________

The Business Scenario: A Common, Real‑World Process

Let’s look at a typical procurement flow:

  1. A Purchase Requisition is created in Manage Purchase Requisitions – Professional

    • A Desired Supplier is maintained
    • A Supplier Material Number (IDNLF) is entered at item level

  2. The requisition is processed in Process Purchase Requisitions

    • A Source of Supply is assigned (matching the Desired Supplier)

  3. A Purchase Order is created using Create Purchase Order

At this point, users expect all relevant and already‑maintained information to be transferred consistently.
However, the resulting Purchase Order does not contain the Supplier Material Number (IDNLF).

 

________________________________________________________________________________________________________

The Gap Today: Information Is Lost Along the Process

The Supplier Material Number is not a cosmetic field—it is often a business‑critical identifier used by suppliers for:

  • Order confirmation
  • Logistics and delivery processing
  • Invoice matching and clarification
  • Communication with supplier purchasing departments

When this field is entered manually in the Purchase Requisition but not transferred to the Purchase Order, important information is lost along an otherwise integrated process.

As a result:

  • Users need to re‑enter data manually
  • The risk of inconsistencies or errors increases
  • Additional clarification with suppliers becomes necessary

This breaks the promise of an end‑to‑end digital procurement process.

________________________________________________________________________________________________________

Why This Matters for Procurement Efficiency

In productive environments, Purchase Requisition processors handle high volumes of requisitions under time pressure. Every additional step reduces efficiency and increases frustration.

The missing transfer of IDNLF leads to:

  • Extra manual steps during PO creation or follow‑up changes
  • Reduced process speed
  • Potential quality issues in supplier communication

Instead of focusing on value‑adding tasks, users are forced to compensate for system limitations.

________________________________________________________________________________________________________

The Opportunity: Small Enhancement, High Value

The proposed improvement is straightforward:

Automatically transfer the Supplier Material Number (IDNLF) from the Purchase Requisition to the Purchase Order when a matching Source of Supply is assigned.

This ensures that:

  • All relevant procurement data is preserved throughout the process
  • The Purchase Order is complete from the moment it is created
  • Manual rework is eliminated

Technically simple, but with a disproportionately positive impact on daily procurement operations.

________________________________________________________________________________________________________

Expected Benefits

By closing this gap, SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud customers benefit from:

  • Improved efficiency and productivity
    Purchase Requisition processors can order faster with fewer steps in between.

  • Enhanced user experience
    Users trust that entered data is reliably carried forward.

  • Higher data quality
    Ensuring that no critical information is lost along the process.

  • Better supplier communication
    Suppliers receive Purchase Orders containing their own material references.

________________________________________________________________________________________________________

Call to Action: Support the Improvement Request

This improvement request is based on real customer usage. If you encounter similar challenges or agree that procurement data should remain consistent from requisition to order, I encourage you to:

👉 Please Vote to Idea # 343655 on SAP Influence and help move this enhancement forward.

entry.title: Custom Logic using Key User Extensibility in SAP S/4 HANA Public Cloud
entry.content:
Introduction

In On Stack extensibility, Enhancements can be created using Custom Logic App in Key User Extensibility.

Key user extensibility also know as In App extensibility, extensions can be added with key user tools.

 

Key User Extensibility / In App Extensibility- Custom Logic

Custom Logic is one of the Key User Extensibility Tools                        

In the Custom logic App, 

  • Custom logic can be added
  • Easy exploration of extension points, key user documentation
  • Syntax check, syntax highlighting, code completion
  • Draft, published, testing included
  • ABAP Language Version for Key Users
  • Enhancements can be created using Custom Logi App

Business Accelerator Hub

SAP Business Accelerator Hub provides a section for On Stack extensibility (key user extensibility and developer extensibility), to know list of SAP CDS views, APIs, Business Add-ins etc. are available with the release status.

Navigate to Business Accelerator Hub to find BAdis

On Stack Extensibility -> Business Add-ins

Search for required BAdi.

This BAdi is related to check purchase order before saving to create it.

Here it shows BAdi Definition and Enhancement spot.

BAdi can be used in both Key User and Developer Extensibility as both states of BAdi is in released state.

Methods and its Parameters

 

Steps to Create Custom Logic

Navigate to Custom logic App

Custom Logic App

Choose Create button to create Custom Logic.

Choose Required Business Content

 

List of available BAdi Definitions for the business context.

Choose required BAdi Definition.

Its Documentation.

Choose Step 2 to add Implementation Description and ID.

Implementation ID automatically appears based on Description and it can also be changed.

Choose Review

Choose Create

Click on Publish button.

Its published.

Click on Open Code Editor to write the logic.

Importing Parameters.

Changing Parameters.

Sampe Code -> Show Sample Code

Keyboard Shortcuts -> three dot menu -> Show Keyboard Shortcuts

Write the required logic.

IF purchaseorder-supplier EQ '0000100005'.
APPEND VALUE #( messagetype = 'E' messagevariable1 = |Choose another Supplier| ) TO messages.
ENDIF.

 Navigate to Create Purchase order (ME21N)

Provide the required details and check.

Error is appeared as per logic written.

This implementation class can be opened in the eclipse, and break point can be added in the logic, and it triggers when creating purchase order in the Create Purchase Order (ME21N).

Key-user objects can be displayed and debugged but cannot be edited with ABAP development tools.

 

Conclusion                     

In Key User Extensibility,

Custom logic can be added and Enhancements can be created using Custom Logic App.

Own implementation can be created and those can be deleted.

Implementation can be published to test, production systems

Code can be compared with older code versions.

 

References

  • Custom Logic | SAP Help Portal
  • Custom Logic Tracing | SAP Help Portal
  • Extensibility | SAP Help Portal
  • Key User Extensibility | SAP Help Portal

entry.title: Enhance Your AI Knowledge in SAP Finance: New Learning Journey Now Available
entry.content:
The future of finance is here, and it's powered by artificial intelligence. Are you ready to lead the charge in AI-driven financial transformation? We're excited to announce the launch of our latest learning journey: Applying Business AI Solutions and Expertise in SAP Finance

Why This Learning Journey Matters

In today's rapidly evolving business landscape, finance professionals who can harness the power of AI aren't just staying competitive; they're becoming indispensable. This intermediate learning journey is designed specifically for the next generation of finance professionals who are ready to take the lead in digital transformation and unlock the full potential of SAP's cutting-edge AI solutions.

What You'll Learn

This learning journey takes you on an immersive exploration of key AI tools that are revolutionizing finance operations:

  • SAP Joule: Discover how this intelligent assistant transforms everyday finance tasks
  • SAP Joule Agents: Learn to leverage specialized AI agents for complex financial processes
  • SAP Joule for Consultants: Master the tools that empower finance consulting excellence
  • SAP Business Data Cloud in Finance: Unlock the potential of intelligent data management

A Hands-On Learning Experience

What sets this learning journey apart is its practical, experience-driven approach. You explore Joule features in Finance firsthand through practical, hands-on exercises that enable learners to experience these capabilities themselves. You'll follow a fictitious consultant's journey as they implement AI tools across various finance business processes, giving you real-world context and actionable insights you can apply immediately.

Starting Your AI Journey? We've Got You Covered

If you're new to AI in finance, we recommend starting with our beginner-level learning journey: Discovering SAP Business AI Capabilities for SAP Finance. This foundational course introduces you to the core concepts and capabilities of AI in the finance domain, providing the perfect stepping stone before diving into the more advanced, hands-on content in our new intermediate learning journey. Whether you're taking your first steps or ready to deepen your expertise, we have a path designed for your learning needs.

Curious?

The intersection of AI and finance represents one of the most exciting opportunities in today's business world. By completing our learning journey, you'll not only gain technical expertise but also position yourself as an expert in the AI-driven future of finance. Start learning right now!

entry.title: DebugSheet: Get XML Data for Local Print Form Preview in the Adobe LiveCycle Designer
entry.content:
Overview of my Print Form Resources

I decided to create a separate article to avoid duplicate content in each print form blog post and simplify the maintenance. You can find the list of print form-related Blogs, information on Live Sessions and an Onboarding Video for beginners in the following blog post:

Print Form Adaptation with Adobe LiveCycle Designer Overview - Onboarding, Blogs and Live Session

 

Intro

When working on print forms in Adobe LiveCycle Designer, having XML data (sometimes called sample data) to preview the form locally is invaluable. Here are some of the key benefits:

  • No need to upload to preview: You can check your current layout and verify that adjustments render correctly — all without uploading the form and opening the target business object in the system again and again after each little adjustment.
  • Field identification: With real sample data from your system, you can compare the output directly to the system screen and locate fields by their content, which is especially helpful when you don’t know a field’s exact technical name.
  • Local data manipulation: You can modify data locally — extend a text, insert line breaks, duplicate items, and so on — to see how the form behaves with different data sets.

Bottom line: Nothing beats real sample data from the target system. 💪

 

How to get XML Data in SAP systems?

The ways to obtain that XML data vary significantly across SAP solutions and often even differ by business object within the same solution. This guide applies to SAP solutions where forms are rendered via the Adobe Document Services (ADS) and customized using Adobe LiveCycle Designer. These include:

  • SAP Sales/Service Cloud (Version 1, formerly SAP Cloud for Customer)
  • SAP Sales/Service Cloud Version 2
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
  • SAP Business ByDesign

The approach described here is often a last resort, as it involves some effort — if a system offers better ways, use those 😉
However, the DebugSheet provides an unfiltered look behind the scenes.

Important: Since this method requires replacing the currently active form template in the system, it should never be used in production environments!

 

Procedure

  1. Copy the content of the XML code block from the end of this blog into a text editor and save the file as DebugSheet2.xdp
    This workaround is necessary because uploading .xdp files is not supported here.
  2. Next, upload the file as the template for the desired document in your system. Here’s where to do that in each solution:
    • SAP Sales/Service Cloud: Work Center Administrator → View Form Template Maintenance
    • SAP Sales/Service Cloud Version 2: System Settings → Form Templates → (All Settings) → (Output Management) → Maintain Form Template
    • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition: Output Management Form Template app
    • SAP Business ByDesign: Work Center Application and User Management → View Form Template Maintenance

  3. Don’t forget to publish/activate the template and, if applicable, configure any rules that ensure the template is actually used for output.
  4. Now navigate to the relevant business object or app in the system and trigger the PDF generation.
    The DebugSheet will appear, displaying the XML data.

 

A word of caution when copying: Copying text from PDF files is generally not as seamless as copying from, say, a Word document. For example, selecting and copying text across multiple pages in a PDF often pulls in unwanted content from headers, footers, or other overlapping elements. The DebugSheet uses a layout that is optimized for copying XML content spanning multiple pages, but you may still encounter issues with truncated or wrapped lines. At the very least, however, you can search for specific fields and their values to verify whether certain values are part of the data set at all and could therefore be displayed in the PDF.

 

Behind the Scenes

Extracting the XML data is no rocket science. The DebugSheet essentially leverages a built-in function and outputs the result in an optimized layout. The following line of JavaScript does the heavy lifting:

this.rawValue = xfa.data.saveXML("pretty");

Here, this should reference a text field that allows line breaks and whose height expands automatically to fit the content.

 

DebugSheet2.xdp

Copy the code block's content to a text editor and save it as DebugSheet2.xdp. This workaround is necessary because uploading .xdp files is not supported here. (Do not use Word or similar programs, since they don't save plain text files.)

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<?xfa generator="AdobeLiveCycleDesigner_V11.0.9.20230825.1.45_SAP" APIVersion="3.6.23237.0"?>
<xdp:xdp xmlns:xdp="http://ns.adobe.com/xdp/" timeStamp="2024-07-10T10:21:11Z" uuid="178558f9-0971-43fe-b129-712041047f6d">
<template xmlns:xliff="urn:oasis:names:tc:xliff:document:1.1" xmlns="http://www.xfa.org/schema/xfa-template/2.8/">
   <?formServer defaultPDFRenderFormat acrobat9.0dynamic?>
   <?formServer allowRenderCaching 0?>
   <?formServer formModel both?>

</script>

               </field>

                  </ui>

                     <exData contentType="text/html" maxLength="0" xliff:rid="959E1C9E-9773-4F4F-9D17-C142F6A50880">
                        <body xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1999/xhtml" xmlns:xfa="http://www.xfa.org/schema/xfa-data/1.0/"><p style="text-decoration:none;letter-spacing:0in">Pages:<span style="xfa-spacerun:yes"> </span><span xfa:embedType="uri" xfa:embedMode="raw" xfa:embed="#floatingField018467"/></p></body>
                     </exData>
                  </value>
                  <font typeface="Arial"/>
                  <margin bottomInset="0mm" leftInset="0mm" rightInset="0mm" topInset="0mm"/>
                  <para vAlign="middle" hAlign="right"/>
               </draw>
               <bind match="none"/>
               <draw name="lblDebugSheet" y="0.154mm" x="0.085mm" w="28.575mm" h="5.2331mm">
                  <ui>
                     <textEdit/>
                  </ui>

                  </value>

               <draw name="lblContact" y="0.154mm" x="35.01mm" w="136.525mm" h="5.2331mm">
                  <ui>
                     <textEdit/>
                  </ui>

                  </value>

                     <fill>
                        <color value="192,192,192"/>
                     </fill>
                  </font>
                  <margin topInset="0.5mm" bottomInset="0.5mm" leftInset="0.5mm" rightInset="0.5mm"/>
                  <para hAlign="center"/>
                  <?renderCache.bounds 384166 12000 0 0 1417 1417 0 0?></draw>
               <?templateDesigner expand 1?></subform>
            <contentArea x="6.35mm" y="12.7mm" w="203.2mm" h="260.35mm"/>
            <medium stock="default" short="215.9mm" long="279.4mm"/>
            <draw name="Line" y="273.05mm" x="6.35mm" w="203.2mm" h="0in">
               <value>
                  <line slope="/"/>
               </value>
            </draw>
            <occur min="1" max="1"/>
            <?templateDesigner expand 0?></pageArea>
         <pageArea name="Page2" id="Page2_ID">
            <medium stock="default" short="612pt" long="792pt"/>
            <contentArea x="6.35mm" y="6.35mm" w="203.2mm" h="266.7mm"/>
            <draw name="Line" y="6.35mm" x="6.351mm" w="203.2mm" h="0in">
               <value>
                  <line slope="/"/>
               </value>
            </draw>
            <draw name="Line" y="273.05mm" x="6.351mm" w="203.2mm" h="0in">
               <value>
                  <line slope="/"/>
               </value>
            </draw>
            <?templateDesigner expand 1?></pageArea>
         <?templateDesigner expand 0?></pageSet>
      <subform w="8in" name="bdy_Page" layout="tb">
